title: Actions, preconditions and effects

publication.changeSummary: Source-linked AI Foundations draft for Actions, preconditions and effects; all claims, examples, metrics, code, safety boundaries, and answer oracles require independent human review. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: CS2023 Artificial Intelligence; August 2023; Planning Systems, Constraint Satisfaction, Decision Making, and Reinforcement Learning foundations; atomic topic: Actions, preconditions and effects
sources.claim: A classical planning action has preconditions defining applicability and effects defining the successor state; a plan is valid when each action applies in sequence and the final state satisfies the goal.

canonicalExplanation.summary: A classical planning action has preconditions defining applicability and effects defining the successor state; a plan is valid when each action applies in sequence and the final state satisfies the goal.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: pick-up(key) requires at(key) and empty-hand, removes at(key), and adds holding(key); the validator simulates every step before testing the door-open goal. Your goal is to encode and validate a small goal-directed planning problem. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: State-space and adversarial search; Knowledge representation and uncertainty; States, actions, goals and costs; Symbols, predicates and rules. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Bounded operational model
sections.body: A classical planning action has preconditions defining applicability and effects defining the successor state; a plan is valid when each action applies in sequence and the final state satisfies the goal. Any capability or quality claim remains limited to an explicit task, population, conditions, baseline, and recorded evidence.

sections.heading: Failure boundary and non-claim
sections.body: Frame assumptions must preserve unaffected facts, real actions can be stochastic or durative, resource conflicts need representation, a valid plan may be costly or unsafe, and omitted effects invalidate simulation. The learner must reject this overclaim: A list of actions is a valid plan whenever its final action mentions the goal, regardless of intermediate preconditions.

includes: Represent a planning domain and distinguish plan validity from plan quality.
Encode and validate a small goal-directed planning problem.
excludes: Frame assumptions must preserve unaffected facts, real actions can be stochastic or durative, resource conflicts need representation, a valid plan may be costly or unsafe, and omitted effects invalidate simulation.

outcomes: Encode and validate a small goal-directed planning problem.

examples.title: Actions, preconditions and effects: bounded direct case
examples.situation: pick-up(key) requires at(key) and empty-hand, removes at(key), and adds holding(key); the validator simulates every step before testing the door-open goal.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the exact data, state, metric, or tool behavior: pick-up(key) requires at(key) and empty-hand, removes at(key), and adds holding(key); the validator simulates every step before testing the door-open goal.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the claim boundary with: Simulate actions from the initial state, require every precondition when used, apply complete effects, and test the explicit goal after the full sequence.
examples.result: This example supports only the atomic outcome because Simulate actions from the initial state, require every precondition when used, apply complete effects, and test the explicit goal after the full sequence.

examples.title: Actions, preconditions and effects: stress and transfer case
examples.situation: Two individually applicable actions compete for one battery resource, so the state adds remaining energy before the plan is judged valid.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Name the changed population, input, assumption, or failure: Two individually applicable actions compete for one battery resource, so the state adds remaining energy before the plan is judged valid.

Step 2 — Keep the non-claim visible: Frame assumptions must preserve unaffected facts, real actions can be stochastic or durative, resource conflicts need representation, a valid plan may be costly or unsafe, and omitted effects invalidate simulation.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting overclaim: A list of actions is a valid plan whenever its final action mentions the goal, regardless of intermediate preconditions.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Encode and validate a small goal-directed planning problem.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: A list of actions is a valid plan whenever its final action mentions the goal, regardless of intermediate preconditions.
misconceptions.correction: Simulate actions from the initial state, require every precondition when used, apply complete effects, and test the explicit goal after the full sequence.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Identify the first unsupported inference in this belief and the evidence required to narrow it: A list of actions is a valid plan whenever its final action mentions the goal, regardless of intermediate preconditions.

analogy.example: a bounded operations desk that checks action preconditions, resource constraints, uncertain outcomes, and proxy rewards before acting can introduce Actions, preconditions and effects only after its canonical technical definition and evidence boundary are stated.
usefulFor: planning, constraints, expected utility, and sequential decisions
limitations: real preferences, uncertainty, stakeholders, safety constraints, and dynamics exceed one plan or reward score

recap.summary: You should now be able to encode and validate a small goal-directed planning problem. Keep this boundary in view: Frame assumptions must preserve unaffected facts, real actions can be stochastic or durative, resource conflicts need representation, a valid plan may be costly or unsafe, and omitted effects invalidate simulation.
retrievalPrompts: Explain Actions, preconditions and effects with one technical mechanism, one evidence requirement, and one non-claim.
Why is this overclaim invalid: A list of actions is a valid plan whenever its final action mentions the goal, regardless of intermediate preconditions.
Apply the boundary to: Two individually applicable actions compete for one battery resource, so the state adds remaining energy before the plan is judged valid.
recap.nextReviewPrompt: Next: continue to “Constraint-satisfaction problems”. Before moving on, answer one retrieval prompt without reopening the explanation.
